fast.summary: A comprehensive survey of how foundation models (LLMs, vision models, etc.) are transforming recommender systems across three paradigms: using FMs to enrich feature representations, generating recommendations directly, and building agentic systems that interactively recommend. It covers data foundations, FM capabilities, and critical trade-offs between paradigms.
fast.key_idea: Foundation models can enhance recommender systems at three levels of integration — as feature extractors, as generative rankers/retrievers, and as autonomous agents — each offering different capability/complexity trade-offs.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners building recommendation systems can leverage this taxonomy to choose the right FM integration strategy for their use case, understanding the maturity, scalability costs, and capability gains of each paradigm before committing to an architecture.
possible_use_cases: E-commerce product recommendation using LLM-based user preference understanding and generative ranking
Content streaming platforms deploying agentic recommenders that clarify user intent through dialogue before surfacing results
Cold-start recommendation for new users or items by leveraging FM world knowledge to bootstrap representations without interaction history

deep.method_breakdown: This survey categorizes FM-powered recommender systems into three paradigms: feature-based augmentation (using FMs like BERT/CLIP to enrich user/item representations without changing the core RS architecture), generative recommendation (FMs directly generate item predictions or ranked lists via next-token prediction or seq2seq frameworks), and agentic systems (LLM-based agents that autonomously plan, retrieve, and interact with users/tools in multi-step recommendation loops). Each paradigm represents increasing integration depth of FMs into the RS pipeline, from peripheral enrichment to full autonomy.
deep.key_innovation: The three-paradigm taxonomy itself is the conceptual contribution — prior surveys either focused narrowly on LLMs for RS or conflated generative and agentic approaches. The explicit separation of agentic RS as a distinct paradigm (where LLMs act as autonomous decision-makers with tool use, memory, and multi-turn interaction) is novel framing that wasn't present in earlier 2022–2023 surveys. The critical trade-off analysis across paradigms (accuracy vs. controllability vs. latency vs. cost) is also more rigorous than prior work.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice highlighted is the tension between ID-based collaborative filtering signals and semantic FM representations — feature-based methods that fuse both (e.g., LLM embeddings + CF embeddings via late fusion) consistently outperform pure-FM approaches on cold-start while retaining warm-user accuracy, because FM semantics capture transferable knowledge but miss interaction-specific behavioral patterns that ID embeddings encode directly.
deep.limitations: As a survey, it inherits the limitation of snapshot coverage — the agentic paradigm especially is evolving faster than survey cycles allow. The paper acknowledges but does not resolve the fundamental evaluation gap: standard RS metrics (NDCG, HR) are ill-suited for agentic systems where conversational quality, planning correctness, and tool-use efficiency matter. Computational cost and latency of FM inference in real-time RS pipelines are discussed qualitatively but not benchmarked. Coverage of non-English and non-e-commerce domains is thin.
interview_questions: What is the fundamental difference between the generative and agentic paradigms in this survey's taxonomy, and why does that distinction matter for system design?
Why do feature-based FM approaches often outperform end-to-end generative FM approaches on standard recommendation benchmarks despite using less FM capability?
What are the key challenges in evaluating agentic recommender systems, and what evaluation frameworks does the survey propose?
How does the survey characterize the cold-start vs. warm-user trade-off when integrating foundation models into recommender systems?
What open research problems does the survey identify as most critical for next-generation FM-powered recommender systems?
interview_answers: Generative RS treats the FM as the recommendation model itself — it inputs user history and outputs item predictions via language generation (e.g., 'the next item is X'). Agentic RS uses the FM as an autonomous agent that decomposes tasks, calls external tools (search, databases, APIs), maintains memory across turns, and interacts with users in multi-step dialogues. The distinction matters because agentic systems require different infrastructure (tool orchestration, memory management), evaluation metrics (task completion, dialogue quality), and raise distinct alignment/controllability concerns.

Feature-based approaches preserve the collaborative filtering backbone that captures behavioral co-occurrence patterns — information not present in FM pre-training data. FM embeddings add semantic generalization (bridging unseen items via text descriptions) but don't encode 'users who bought X also bought Y' signals that are highly predictive for warm users. End-to-end generative FM approaches must learn these patterns from scratch during fine-tuning on typically small RS datasets, while feature-based fusion gets both for free.

The survey identifies three key evaluation challenges: (1) standard IR metrics like NDCG measure single-step relevance but not multi-turn conversational quality or planning correctness; (2) there is no standard benchmark for agentic RS — most papers use custom simulated environments that don't transfer; (3) side effects of autonomous agents (unintended tool calls, hallucinated item attributes) are not captured by accuracy metrics. The survey suggests adopting LLM-as-judge evaluation, task-completion rate in simulated user environments, and human evaluation for conversational naturalness.

FMs help most on cold-start because they can leverage item/user text metadata to make reasonable predictions without interaction history — semantic similarity from pre-training transfers well. For warm users with rich interaction history, traditional CF signals are highly predictive and FMs add marginal value or can even hurt by introducing noise. The optimal integration strategy is therefore conditional: use FM representations heavily for new users/items, and blend them with CF signals (weighted or via gating mechanisms) for established users.

The survey identifies five critical open problems: (1) efficient FM inference at RS serving latency constraints (sub-100ms) — current LLM inference is orders of magnitude too slow for real-time ranking; (2) alignment of FM-generated recommendations with business constraints (diversity, fairness, inventory) without explicit reward engineering; (3) continual learning — FMs are static post-training but user preferences and item catalogs change continuously; (4) grounding and hallucination prevention — generative models fabricate item attributes or non-existent items; (5) privacy-preserving FM fine-tuning since RS data contains sensitive behavioral signals.
